060243123 Computer Network and Internet
Week 2 • Chapter 1

Fundamentals of Computer Networks

This week introduces how data is communicated, how devices connect, what media carry signals, and how the Internet evolved.

Topics in this week

  1. Data Communication Components
  2. Data Flow
  3. Physical Structure
  4. Network Topology
  1. Network Types
  2. Hierarchical Network Architecture
  3. Transmission Media
  4. Internet History

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 1
Week 2 foundation

What are computer networks, and why do they matter?

What is a computer network?

A computer network is a group of computers, servers, smartphones, printers, and other devices connected so they can communicate and share resources.

These devices may connect through cables, Wi‑Fi, optical fiber, or the Internet, but they must follow shared communication rules called protocols.

Computer networks communicate through a data communication system. In other words, the network is the whole connected environment, while data communication is the process of sending data from one device to another inside that network.

Example of a computer network

The moving dots represent data traveling between connected devices and network equipment.

Why do computer networks matter?

💬

Communication

They enable messaging, email, online learning, meetings, and teamwork across locations.

📁

Resource sharing

They allow users to share files, printers, storage, software, databases, and services.

☁️

Digital services

Websites, mobile apps, cloud, IoT, and AI services depend on computer networks.

🛠️

Problem solving

They help explain slow connections, disconnections, security risks, and unavailable services.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 2
Data Communication Components

Five components of data communication

Click a component in the diagram

A complete data communication system requires a sender, receiver, message, transmission medium, and protocol working together.

Classroom focus: A computer network provides the connected environment; data communication is the actual sending and receiving process inside it.

📤

Sender

Creates, formats, and prepares data for transmission. It may add control information so the destination can understand it.

📥

Receiver

Receives signals, converts them back into data, checks correctness, and passes data to the destination application.

📝

Message

The information being sent: text, numbers, images, audio, or video. It may become packets, frames, or segments.

🛣️

Medium

The channel data travels through, such as copper cable, optical fiber, Wi‑Fi, radio wave, or infrared.

📜

Protocol

The rules for format, timing, error checking, and understanding, such as TCP/IP, HTTP, FTP, and SMTP.

Remember: In real communication, roles can switch. For example, a browser sends a request, then the web server sends a response.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 3
Applied example

Five components example: sending a Messenger message

No scenario selected yet. Click Scenario A or Scenario B to activate the flow and highlight.

Select a scenario to begin

No scenario selected

Click Scenario A or Scenario B first to show the direction of communication.

After that: click Sender, Message, Medium, Protocol, or Receiver to highlight the matching object on the left.

Highlight guide: click a component to see a yellow highlight on the left.
Direction matters: sender and receiver switch when the reply goes back.
Medium + Protocol: the medium carries data; protocols define how it is understood.
Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 4
Protocol intuition

Protocol = agreed rules between Side A and Side B

🍽️

Everyday-life protocol: ordering food

Side ACustomer
Side BWaiter / restaurant

Both sides follow shared rules so the order is understood correctly.

Start: greet and ask to order
Format: item, size, quantity, options
Check: repeat the order to avoid mistakes
End: confirm, pay, and receive the food
🌐

Network protocol: sending data

Side ASender / client
Side BReceiver / server

Devices follow shared rules so data has the correct meaning, order, destination, and response.

Start: begin a connection or request
Format: organize data into messages or packets
Check: detect errors and confirm delivery
End: close the session or send a response

Examples of network protocols

HTTP / HTTPS

Rules for web request and response. HTTPS protects web communication using encryption.

TCP

Reliable delivery using ordering, acknowledgement, and retransmission.

IP

Addressing and routing so packets can travel across different networks.

DNS

Translates a domain name, such as a website name, into an IP address.

SMTP / FTP

SMTP sends email. FTP transfers files between devices.

Key idea: A protocol is not the data itself. It is the rule set that tells both sides how to send, receive, check, and understand the data.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 5
Data Flow

Data flow direction

Data flow is the direction of data transmission between two devices. It explains whether data moves only one way, takes turns, or moves both ways at the same time.

➡️

Simplex

One-way transmission only. The receiver does not send data back.

Examples: keyboard → computer, TV broadcast, sensor → display.

↔️

Half Duplex

Two-way communication, but only one side sends at a time.

Examples: walkie-talkie, push-to-talk radio, shared radio channel.

🔁

Full Duplex

Both sides can send and receive at the same time.

Examples: phone call, video call, switched Ethernet.

Device A
Device B

Simplex

Data flows only from A to B. Suitable for broadcasting that does not require a reply.

Simplex example: TV broadcast, keyboard → computer, sensor → display.

Why it matters: Data flow affects throughput, latency, signal collision, and how devices take turns using the communication medium.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 6
Physical Structure

Physical connection structure

Physical structure describes whether the communication path is dedicated to two devices or shared by many devices.

🔗

Point-to-Point

One dedicated link connects exactly two devices.

Examples: PC–switch, router–router, Bluetooth pair Good: stable, private, easy to trace Limit: many devices need many links
🕸️

Multipoint

Three or more devices share the same medium.

Examples: classroom Wi‑Fi, shared wireless channel Good: supports many devices, easy to expand Limit: needs sharing rules; may be congested

Quick practice

Click a situation.

Click a scenario to see the answer.
Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 7

Network topology

Network topology is the arrangement of devices and links in a network. It shows how devices are connected and how data can move from one device to another.

Sender = start nodeReceiver = destination nodeOrange packet = normal trafficRed packet / X = failed or stopped trafficGreen dashed path = backup routeBlue line = active cable/link
Only one moving dot is shown at a time so students can follow the path clearly.

Select a topology

Star Topology

All devices connect to a central device such as a switch.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 8
Design guide

Choosing a suitable topology from real situations

How to decide: identify the devices, number of users, reliability need, future growth, and what happens if one node or link fails.

Device icons
🖥️ PCs🖨️ Printer🗄️ Server🔀 Switch

Computer lab / office

Recommended: Star

Each device connects to one central switch. This is the most common LAN design because it is simple to manage and troubleshoot.

Failure idea: one PC failure affects only that device; switch failure affects the whole group.

Critical system / backbone

Recommended: Mesh / Hybrid

Use multiple paths so traffic can still move when one link fails. Suitable for critical services and backbone networks.

Failure idea: if one path breaks, traffic may reroute through another available path.

Many departments / buildings

Recommended: Tree / Hierarchical

Organizes the network into levels, such as core, distribution, and access. This structure is suitable for larger organizations.

Failure idea: an upper-level failure may affect many lower branches.

Small low-cost experiment

Possible: Bus

Simple and uses less cable, but mainly useful for understanding older shared-medium networks rather than modern LAN design.

Failure idea: if the backbone breaks, communication across the bus may stop.

Teaching point: choose a topology based on the situation, devices, scalability, and failure impact — not only by the shape of the diagram.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 9
Network Types

Network types by coverage area

LAN
PANLANMANWANInternet
Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 10
From small to global

Understanding network scale with a global map

Simple view: network coverage grows from one place to the whole world
Reading tip: the colored dots now grow more clearly with the coverage scale: LAN is smallest and local, MAN is larger for a city or campus, WAN spans distant regions, and the Internet is the largest because it connects networks worldwide.

LAN Local Area Network

Very small scale. Connects nearby devices in one room, lab, office, or building. Usually managed by one organization.

MAN Metropolitan Area Network

City or campus scale. Connects several LANs in one area, such as multiple buildings in a university or offices across one city.

WAN Wide Area Network

Regional or national scale. Connects distant branches through carrier services, leased lines, MPLS, VPNs, or ISP infrastructure.

Internet Global scale

Worldwide scale. It is the largest example, where many WANs, ISPs, cloud providers, and local networks interconnect globally.

Key idea for learners

Network type tells us how far the network covers. Topology tells us how devices or sites are connected inside that network. So the scale and the topology are related, but they are not the same concept.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 11
Specialized networks

Specialized networks to know

These network types serve more specific purposes. PAN focuses on one person and personal devices, WLAN gives wireless access inside a local area, and SAN is designed for high-speed storage connections in server environments.

PAN

Personal Area Network covers a very short distance around one person, usually linking personal devices.

CoverageAbout 1–10 meters around a user
Common technologiesBluetooth, NFC, USB, tethering
ExamplePhone connected to smartwatch, earbuds, and tablet

WLAN

Wireless LAN is a local network that uses Wi‑Fi instead of network cables for end users.

CoverageA room, floor, home, classroom, or office
Common technologiesWi‑Fi access point, wireless router, IEEE 802.11
ExampleStudents connect laptops and phones to classroom Wi‑Fi

SAN

Storage Area Network is a specialized high-speed network that links servers to shared storage.

CoverageUsually inside a server room or data center
Common technologiesFibre Channel, iSCSI, storage switches, disk arrays
ExampleUniversity servers access a central storage system for backup and databases

Quick comparison: PAN is the smallest and most personal, WLAN provides wireless local connectivity for many users, and SAN is not about user coverage area but about efficient and reliable storage connectivity.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 12
Hierarchical Network Architecture

Hierarchical network architecture

Why use layers?

Large networks are easier to design and troubleshoot when each part has a clear responsibility.

Access Layer

Connects end devices such as PCs, printers, phones, IoT devices, and wireless access points.

Distribution Layer

Aggregates access switches and applies routing, VLAN control, filtering, and security policies.

Core Layer

Provides fast and reliable backbone connections between buildings, data centers, and Internet links.

Classroom idea: Think of a university: each room/floor is Access, each building is Distribution, and the campus backbone is Core.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 13
Hierarchical Network Architecture

Click each layer to see its role.

Core Layer backbone / high speed
Distribution Layer routing / policy
Access Layer end users / devices

Access Layer

Connects end-user devices to the network. This is the first network layer that users normally interact with.

Common devicesPrinter, IP phone, wireless AP, access switch
Main focusHow local users and devices enter the network

Access layer device photos

Click image to view front and back
Printer
1× Ethernet, 1× USB
IP Phone
LAN + PC ports
Wireless AP
1× Ethernet/PoE
Access Switch
24× RJ45 + uplinks
Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 14
Transmission Media

Transmission media

Transmission medium = the path that carries a signal. In real networks, devices may use UTP, optical fiber, Wi‑Fi, Bluetooth, infrared, or mobile / wide-area wireless depending on the situation.

Media types
Physical path

🔌 Guided media

Signals travel through a physical path such as UTP, coaxial cable, and optical fiber.

🔌UTP cable
✨Fiber optic
Through air / wireless

📡 Unguided media

Signals travel through the air such as Wi‑Fi, Bluetooth, infrared, and mobile wireless.

Guided and unguided media examples
🖥️PC
🖨️Printer
🔀Switch
📶AP / Wireless
📱Phone
Wi‑Fi Router
🏢Building
🗄️Data Center
ISPService Provider
🛰️Satellite / WAN link
Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 16
Guided media

Wired transmission media

UTP Ethernet

Common copper cable for classroom, office, and LAN connections.

Coaxial Cable

Layered cable with shielding, often used in cable TV and broadband access.

Optical Fiber

Uses light to carry data for high speed and long-distance links.

UTP Ethernet

UTP is the most common LAN cable for connecting PCs, printers, access points, and switches.

Best forClassrooms, offices, labs, fixed LAN devices
StrengthLow cost, easy installation, flexible cable management
LimitationShorter distance than fiber and more affected by interference
Student checkLook for RJ45 connectors and twisted wire pairs inside
Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 17
Unguided media

Wireless transmission media

Radio Wave / Wi‑Fi

Used for WLAN access and mobile users in classrooms, homes, and offices.

Microwave

Used for point-to-point wireless links between buildings or towers.

Infrared

Short-range directional communication used in remotes and sensors.

Radio Wave / Wi‑Fi

Wi‑Fi uses radio waves to connect mobile devices to a local network through an access point.

Best forPhones, laptops, tablets, and mobile users
StrengthMobility and convenient network access without cables
LimitationObstacles, interference, distance, and user density affect performance
Student checkIdentify AP placement, coverage area, and possible interference
Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 18
Decision helper

Which transmission medium should we choose?

Click a scenario. The recommendation should match the distance, speed, mobility, environment, and cost requirements.

🏢

Connect distant buildings

Needs long distance, high speed, and stability.

🖥️

Computer lab

Many fixed computers in one room.

📶

Student area

Many mobile devices and changing users.

🔴

Short-range control

Nearby devices with direct line-of-sight.

Click a scenario to see the recommendation.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 19
Internet History

Internet history and development

Research networks

Early networking connected large computers and research institutions before the public web existed.

ARPANET

Early research networks demonstrated packet switching and remote communication between institutions. This idea became a foundation for internetworking.

Main ideaBreak data into packets and send them across links.
Why it mattersLarge networks can share resources across distance.
Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 20
Internet today and future

Today’s Internet: from connection to services

The modern Internet is more than web browsing. It connects people, devices, data centers, applications, cloud platforms, and intelligent services.

☁️ Cloud platforms

Applications and storage run in data centers and are accessed on demand through the Internet.

📱 Mobile access

Phones, tablets, and laptops use Wi‑Fi and cellular networks for everyday online services.

🏠 IoT devices

Sensors, cameras, and smart devices send data to applications and cloud services.

🧠 AI services

AI tools often depend on cloud computing, APIs, large data centers, and fast networks.

1. User / DeviceOpens an app, website, sensor, or AI tool.
2. Access NetworkUses Wi‑Fi, LAN, mobile network, or ISP connection.
3. Cloud / ServerProcesses requests, stores data, and runs services.
4. ResponseReturns content, results, alerts, or AI output to the user.
Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod

Activities & Review

Activity 1: Match the network terms

Drag each term into the correct category. After matching, ask students to explain why the term belongs there.

How to do it

Step 1: Drag a term from the left. Step 2: Drop it into the best category. Step 3: Check the feedback and discuss.

Terms to drag

Sender Half Duplex Mesh WAN Optical Fiber Protocol Star Wi‑Fi LAN Full Duplex

Drop zones

Data communication component
Data flow mode
Network topology
Network type
Transmission medium
Drag a term to a category.
Tip: A component is part of data communication, such as sender or protocol.
Tip: A flow describes communication direction, such as Half Duplex or Full Duplex.
Tip: A type or topology describes the network by scale or arrangement.

Think about coverage, number of users, mobility, and cost.
State the topology, the medium, and the key devices such as switch, AP, or router.
Try to justify why your design is better than at least one alternative.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 24
Activities & Review

Chapter summary

1. Communication basics

Data communication needs sender, receiver, message, medium, and protocol. Data may flow in simplex, half duplex, or full duplex mode.

2. Physical connection

Point-to-point and multipoint describe the physical structure, while topology describes how nodes are arranged and connected.

3. Network scale

PAN, LAN, MAN, WAN, and the Internet mainly differ by coverage scale. Specialized networks such as WLAN and SAN support specific purposes.

4. Hierarchical design

Large networks are easier to manage when they are divided into Access, Distribution, and Core layers.

5. Transmission media

Guided media use a physical path such as UTP or fiber. Unguided media use wireless signals such as Wi‑Fi, microwave, and infrared.

6. Big picture

A good network design matches user needs, environment, performance, cost, and future growth.

Instructor: Pitpimon Choorod
Slide 25
Activities & Review

Practice, discussion, and export

In-class or homework tasks

1) Compare topologiesCompare Star and Mesh in terms of reliability, cost, scalability, and troubleshooting.
2) Choose a mediumIf an academic building must connect to a data center, which medium should be selected and why?
3) Plan Wi‑FiWhat problems may occur if too many students use one access point at the same time?
4) Give real examplesGive one realistic example of PAN, LAN, MAN, WAN, and Internet from daily life or university context.
5) Map the hierarchyMap a university network into Access, Distribution, and Core layers.
